metric_value: Leading Intel server suppliers now shipping I2O-Ready products
notes: Aberdeen confirms I2O-Ready labeling appearing on production Intel architecture servers in 1997

metric_value: I2O will be an important functional capability for servers in the near future
notes: Aberdeen predicts I2O becomes near-mandatory server capability

metric_value: I2O failed to achieve broad adoption; I2O SIG dissolved; standard abandoned by early 2000s
notes: I2O never achieved mainstream OS adoption; Windows NT/2000 support was minimal; I/O bottleneck solved via PCIe and multicore

metric_value: Acquiring I2O-Ready should be a requirement for IS planners upgrading OS or buying new Intel servers
notes: Aberdeen strongly recommends I2O-Ready as procurement criterion for servers and OS upgrades

metric_value: I2O-Ready never became a mainstream procurement criterion; label disappeared from server specifications by 2002
notes: Aberdeen's recommendation proved too early; I2O-Ready disappeared from procurement discussions without widespread adoption

metric_value: Steering committee includes 3Com Compaq HP Intel Microsoft NetFRAME Novell Symbios Logic
notes: I2O SIG had broad industry backing from major server and OS vendors

metric_value: Demand for device driver portability and intelligent distributed I/O processing drives I2O development
notes: I2O addresses real I/O bottleneck challenge on Intel architecture servers

metric_value: PCI bus bandwidth is insufficient for high-performance server I/O workloads
notes: I/O bottleneck on IA servers primarily caused by PCI bus and driver proliferation

metric_value: PCI-Express (PCIe) released 2004 with dramatically higher bandwidth; solved problem I2O tried to address
notes: PCIe became the dominant solution to PCI bandwidth bottleneck; I2O approach was bypassed

metric_value: I2O separates OS-specific Module Service Module (OSM) from device Hardware Device Module (HDM)
notes: Core I2O architectural innovation: split driver model for OS independence

metric_value: Windows OS upgrades cited as I2O adoption driver; next releases or service patches will support I2O
notes: Aberdeen implies Windows OS upgrade cycle will drive I2O adoption

metric_value: Windows 2000 included limited I2O support but it was not widely used; Windows XP/Server 2003 effectively dropped I2O
notes: Microsoft's Windows Driver Model (WDM) and later KMDF/UMDF replaced the I2O approach in Windows

metric_value: Novell NetWare 5 (1998) included I2O support and was the primary OS where I2O achieved any adoption
notes: I2O found most success in NetWare environments; limited elsewhere

metric_value: I2O is the key enabler for I/O on IA-64 platforms; develop drivers now for IA-64 compatibility
notes: Aberdeen/I2O SIG positioned I2O as prerequisite for IA-64 (Itanium) readiness

metric_value: IA-64 (Itanium) failed to achieve mainstream server adoption; I2O IA-64 relevance never materialized
notes: Intel Itanium largely failed in commercial market; I2O's IA-64 rationale thus also failed
